data.title: KNG Potentially Safer Than JEPI
data.description:
Introduction

Regular readers of mine will know that I have been an evangelist for the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI), most recently issuing it a strong buy recently, mostly because its holdings are value-oriented, and are likely to be less volatile than the S&P 500 more broadly. I praised it for its low allocation to tech firms, and its general tendency toward prudence from management regarding the short side of the options selling the fund does to raise income.

Enter the FT Vest S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Target Income ETF (BATS:KNG), which promises to be very defensive. Its goal is to own the "dividend aristocrats" of the S&P 500 and sell options against them (individual stocks, not an index) to generate income 8% over the S&P 500's dividend.

Before we get further into the article, it's important to note why I bring up JEPI here. JEPI is the "standard" of derivative income ETFs, and has been both the gold standard of the category in AUM and in my ratings. If KNG can stack up to it from a strategy perspective, that would be a very good look for KNG and could make it a recommendation over JEPI depending on the investor and their goal.

In today's market, where's some funds are finding it hard to stand out, it's not enough to have a compelling strategy on its own, it has to be competitive with what we currently have as well.

Performance Comparison

Over the last few years, we've seen the fund perform admirably against both JEPI and the long-only ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL), which shows us how the fund should've performed without its options selling component. We can see that the elevated income only cost returns slightly.

Speaking of income, let's take a look at the aristocrats + 8% yield that KNG is attempting to create and how it stacks up to these two other funds. After all, while NOBL is not an income fund per se, JEPI is. JEPI actively sells calls against the S&P 500 index, not individual stocks like KNG does (more on that in the next section).

That being said, we've seen that since 2024, KNG has been raising its dividend to its current high of 8.5%. Compare this to JEPI's sub-8%, although during 2022 and 2023 it was closer to 12%, and you have a fund that is very competitive from a current yield standpoint.

KNG vs. JEPI Holdings

The two funds hold a much smaller pool of stocks than the S&P 500 (just an assumed base equity position in a portfolio), with JEPI holding around 115 and KNG holding 70.

Here are their top holdings head-to-head. Note that both funds tend to aim for equal weighting.

After looking at the sector split, I believe that KNG is more conservative than JEPI. KNG has a far lower exposure to the highest volatility sectors (those with firms that have a harder time becoming dividend aristocrats due to their age or volatile financial picture) like technology.

While JEPI is low on tech compared to the S&P 500, it's high on tech compared to KNG.

KNG vs. JEPI Derivatives

One of the major differences between these two funds is the way they sell options. KNG sells individual options, while JEPI utilizes ELNs.

KNG sells options on individual stocks in its portfolio, much closer to DIVO's strategy. The advantage of this strategy is that it allows more flexibility from the managers, where they can cut back on being short certain stocks (or avoid it all together) if they believe there is more upside to be had.

On the other hand, JEPI sells the S&P 500 index short via in-house products made by another JPMorgan team. This means that the fund doesn't incur much counter-party risk (or rather, that it matches the issuer risk). If you want to read more about JEPI's ELNs specifically, I covered them in far more detail here.

Here are the major comparisons I believe investors should consider between these two strategies:

Single Stock Options ELNs
More flexibility for stock upside. Holdings are not directly capped.
Stability of income. Higher income overall.
Long & short the same stocks. Long & short different stocks.
Income is RoC. Income is NII.

Don't worry, I'll discuss the last part in the next section.

KNG vs. JEPI Dividends & Taxes

From a dividend standpoint, we see a lot more variance in JEPI, since it derives its income from the S&P 500 index. KNG derives its income from the individual names that it owns, which are less volatile than the index (and less volatile than JEPI's holdings, which are themselves less volatile than the S&P 500 as well), meaning that the variance in its dividend income is much lower.

While this does generally mean less income overall, especially in high-volatility environments like we're in currently, it does mean that income for KNG is more predictable.

From a tax perspective, I've made the argument in the past that JEPI is not as tax-efficient as it could be because of its use of ELNs, which are tools that reduce some risk JEPI would gain from its short position, but also introduce risks such as tax inefficiencies.

Here is where we're bringing back that chart from the previous section:

When looking at the two funds, they distribute income differently. Because JEPI uses ELNs, I wrote that its income is primarily "NII," or "net investment income." These distributions are considered ordinary income in the year you received them for tax purposes. On the other hand, KNG's income is primarily "RoC" or "return-of-capital," which is tax deferred.

RoC reduces an investor's cost-basis, and taxes are realized as capital gains when the shares are sold. This allows investors to defer their taxes, and realize the gains when they want, despite the income hitting their account monthly. This feature is very handy for investors needing to generate income outside of tax-advantaged accounts.

Note that if your cost basis hits zero, future distributions are categories as long-term capital gains in the year you received the distribution.

Because KNG's distributions are RoC and JEPI's are largely NII, this makes JEPI inefficient from a tax perspective, while KNG is very efficient. This could be a large factor in choosing between these two ETFs for tax-sensitive investors.

Fees

There are only a few other aspects that should be pointed out here. Namely, there are investors who are very fee-sensitive. I am not one of them, and routinely advocate high fee funds if I believe that fee is justified. I feel that way about both of these funds, and believe that they earn their fee. That being said, it is of note that JEPI's fee is below market average, while KNG's is above.

For reference, the average for the category is 0.5%.

Suitability

I view KNG is more conservative than JEPI despite its smaller holdings. While there are fewer of them in KNG, they are broadly from more defensive sectors and from companies with very long histories of raising dividends (25 years minimum!) compared to JEPI's already moderate holdings.

For the record, I am still recommending JEPI to all investors. In the past, I have recommended:

...JEPI could be considered for up to 30% of an aggressive investor's portfolio.

However, conservative and moderate investors would be better served by diversifying some of their JEPI position into KNG, as it allows for a defensive and conservative tilt on the JEPI strategy.

Aggressive investors who are tax-sensitive should consider KNG over JEPI for its tax-efficient nature, but also should consider S&P 500 covered call funds outright, like SPYI.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I still believe that the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF is a great pick for riding out our current market volatility, and still believe it to be more prudent than holding the S&P 500 outright.

However, the FT Vest S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Target Income ETF offers investors access to a more conservative strategy than JEPI from an equity holdings perspective, one which only owns 25+ year dividend payers (and growers), and sells calls in a more tax-efficient manner than JEPI.

This gives investors options, and provides a potentially safer choice for their equity premium exposure in KNG, and opens the discussion to adding KNG exposure to an income portfolio, or diversifying from a JEPI/SPYI allocation to add in more quality.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Dollar On The Ledge - Is A Shove Coming?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

The US dollar is on the ledge. The only question now: Is a shove coming?

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day showing the ICE US Dollar Index (DXY) from a long-term perspective. It’s not pretty.

DXY just knifed through 100 to the downside. It’s now testing key support that dates back the last few years. Moreover, its year-to-date loss of around 6% puts the greenback on track for its worst annual performance since 2017 (though yes, there’s plenty of time until New Year’s Eve!)

I talked about the main reasons why the dollar is diving in my April 11 column here. But even though they’re more widely known and accepted now... and even though selling has been very aggressive since January... I haven’t seen a key reversal or massive flush on the shorter-term chart (below).

That means it’s (still) hard to lean against the trend as a trader. And if you’re a longer-term investor worried about foreign dumping of US assets hurting stocks and bonds? Well, you’re not seeing much that's encouraging here... yet. Even with investor sentiment in the dumpster and contrarian plays looking more tempting because of it!

Bottom line: It’s probably best to hold your fire before getting aggressive to the long side.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Twin Disc Shares Hammered By Trade Uncertainties And Nervous Investors
data.description:
The word "uncertainty" is likely to feature prominently not just in this article, but in many (if not most) articles these days about manufacturing companies with meaningful international exposure. The market being what it is, uncertainty often means selling out of riskier positions and seeking out safer havens until things calm down. That's certainly been the case for Twin Disc (NASDAQ:TWIN). Although the business has held up well so far, and management's commentary with fiscal second quarter earnings (reported early February) was generally positive, investors are clearly worried about how tariff-driven trade wars and slower global economy growth may impact this small manufacturer of marine and heavy duty power transmission equipment.

Shares are down about 40% since my last update, far worse than the typical industrial, and worse than not only rival manufacturers, but also major customers across marine and heavy-duty markets (where the declines have been more on the order of 5% to 15%). It's tempting to see this sell-off as an opportunity to benefit from a market overreaction, but with so much uncertainty in its end-markets and with global trade policy, I'm reluctant to actively encourage stepping up right now.

Where Is Demand Heading? Nobody Really Seems To Know, But Probably Not "Up" Right Away

As I said above, caution and uncertainty are common words to see and hear these days, as companies try to navigate a new global economic landscape that seems to change every couple of days. It's arguably worse for Twin Disc, given that the products the company sells typically end up in high-value equipment where purchasing decisions are driven by rates and economic outlooks (as opposed to consumables that are more driven by current usage volumes).

Twin Disc's Marine business (close to two-thirds of revenue in fiscal Q2'25) is split between commercial craft (mostly tugs, ferries, offshore supply vessels and other workboats), pleasure craft (yachts), and patrol boats.

The outlook for patrol boats is still healthy, as governments (particularly in Asia) continue to spend on upgraded and expanded fleets, but it is not implausible to me that Twin Disc could face increased scrutiny and competition if governments decide to respond to U.S. trade policies by minimizing their purchases from U.S.-based companies.

Other marine categories are more mixed. Most of the major yacht companies, including Beneteau (OTCPK:BTEAF), Ferretti (9638.HK), and Sanlorenzo (OTCPK:SNLRF), sound fairly confident about the longer-term outlook for their luxury products and are going into 2025 with healthy backlogs, but almost all have sounded notes of caution on issues like the European economy and the potential impact of tariffs.

As far as the commercial market goes, economic uncertainty, relatively high rates, and a lack of shipyard capacity is discouraging newbuild activity in most areas, and current energy prices certainly don't help incentivize new-builds for markets like offshore supply vessels.

Looking at Twin Disc's non-marine markets, the airport, rescue, and fire fighting segment still looks pretty healthy, and with demand driven by municipalities, there is less risk here tied to economic uncertainty (particularly as higher housing prices are working their way through into high property taxes and better-supported municipal budgets). I still see a risk of plateauing orders after a period of strength, but Oshkosh (OSK) and REV Group (REVG) seem to be doing relatively well.

I'm less bullish on other markets like agriculture, construction, and recycling/waste handling equipment, as well as fracking. Higher rates, lower commodity prices, and trade wars are not good for U.S. demand for ag equipment, and I likewise see a real risk that ongoing economic uncertainty will weigh on construction activity and new equipment demand. As far as fracking is concerned, activity was already soft back in late 2024 and weakening oil prices are making several U.S. basins less competitive, further limiting near-term capex spending.

Self-Help Is Badly Needed, And I'm Less Confident Here

As I've said in other recent articles on Twin Disc, one of my biggest concerns with recommending the stock is that the company has a relatively poor track record when it comes to sustained revenue growth and margin leverage. The company made strategic moves that should help this - including acquiring more high-value products (the Veth deal), further diversifying the business through M&A, and looking to focus on higher-value systems - but "should" only gets you so far.

Looking back at the fiscal second quarter, gross margin was down about four points year over year and more than two points sequentially, and even adjusting for some write-downs still leaves a two-point year-over-year decline and a slight sequential decline. It's a little better at the EBITDA and operating income lines, with year-over-year margin improvements on adjusted numbers, but management themselves acknowledged that they need to drive more operational efficiencies to improve margins.

Tariffs are going to be a looming risk for the near term. Although Twin Disc has a diverse sales base (over 40% of sales to Europe, nearly one-quarter to Asia, and about one-quarter in North America) and a diverse manufacturing footprint (with plants in the U.S. and several European countries), retaliatory tariffs remain a risk, as do higher input costs in the United States. Management has not quantified this risk, and I'm not sure how they could, given the frequent changes in tariff policy, but none of this is going to be beneficial to margins in the near term.

I just don't have the confidence that self-help can offset these near-term pressures. I like the company's leverage to higher-value systems for yachts, but I'm worried that that market is slowing. Likewise, I like the long-term leverage to electric and hybrid systems for marine customers, but I don't expect that to be a major driver over the next 12-24 months.

The Outlook

The first two quarters of fiscal 2025 have been about what I expected to see, so I'm reluctant to make major changes to my model at this point. That said, tariffs and macro uncertainties do appear to be having a negative impact on global economic activity, and I've modestly trimmed my revenue expectations (by about 2% to 3%) as a result.

I've likewise pulled back my margin expectations, as I think it will be harder to generate gross margin leverage in the current environment and I think that will weigh on EBITDA and operating margin leverage. I'm still expecting EBITDA margins in the 8%-9% range, but I'm not willing to model double-digit margins in the near term without more evidence of a sustained improvement.

All told, I'm still looking for long-term revenue growth around 4%, as I do like the company's efforts to diversify its business into adjacent products/markets (like the recent acquisition of Kobelt) and I think the yacht business is fundamentally a good one for a company like Twin Disc. I'm still skeptical that Twin Disc can get free cash flow margins much above 3% to 5% on a sustained basis, and even the low end of that range is a marked improvement over its long-term averages.

The market expects even less from Twin Disc, as both my discounted cash flow and margin/return-driven EV/EBITDA (using a 6.5x forward multiple) valuation approaches still get me to fair values in the double-digits. To that end, I'd note that Twin Disc is trading at around 4x my 12-month EBITDA estimate - which is about as bad as it gets for manufacturing companies that aren't distressed, but here again I note that for small, uncovered companies, market sell-offs can drive extremely low multiples.

The Bottom Line

The biggest issue I have with recommending Twin Disc now is that there are many higher-quality companies that have also sold off in recent weeks/months and offer risk/reward profiles that depend less upon significant self-improvement relative to historical performance. Whether or not the company can hit its own self-improvements remains a key unknown, and this newly heightened uncertainty around U.S. policies and the global economy doesn't help.

This may well prove to be an excellent buying opportunity, but I still see risks that key end-markets like yachts, commercial marine vessels, and land-based heavy-duty machinery could weaken further, and that the company's margin structure may come under further pressure. It's certainly a candidate for a watch list, but I need to see more data on where margins and key end-markets are heading before getting more bullish.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: APA Corp. Has Become Undervalued Considering Its Top Permian Assets And The GranMorgu Project
data.description:
Introduction

APA Corporation (NASDAQ:APA) is an independent E&P company that operates across various key regions, including the Permian Basin, Egypt, and the North Sea. Over the past year, the company has acquired Callon Petroleum for $4.5B and also started the first phase of its GranMorgu project in Suriname. The E&P currently has a market cap of $5.5B, making the company one of the smallest in the S&P 500.

With deteriorating dynamics for O&G companies, I want to see if APA is worth the risk of WTI prices falling further with the company's high debt load despite strong FCF generation.

Operation Overview

APA has been operating in the Permian and New Mexico since the 1990s, according to the company's website. As of November 2024, APA operates 8 drilling rigs in the region focused on the Midland and Delaware Basins, with an emphasis on drilling longer lateral wells and optimizing sites to maintain high productivity. With the Callon acquisition, APA expanded its footprint in these two regions by adding a total of 145,000 net acres aimed at focusing on higher-margin areas. At the same time, the company sold $950 million worth of non-core legacy assets as part of a portfolio optimization strategy, which should concentrate capital on high-margin, unconventional plays. These transactions have increased APA's onshore U.S. production by around 66,000 BOE/d, now accounting for 313,227 BOE/d in the U.S.

Looking at Egypt, APA is the largest oil producer and acreage holder in the country. The company averaged 12 drilling rigs in the western desert. APA also has 20 workover rigs as it continues to align its drilling and workover activity to drive capital efficiency. The company entered into a new pricing agreement for incremental gas volumes produced in Egypt, making gas exploration and development more economically competitive with oil development. Egypt is responsible for 141,741 BOE/d of reported production, with a blend of 65% Oil and 35% Gas. APA also stated that the company had significant waterflood opportunities across Egypt's asset footprint, mitigating decline for the remaining term of the PSC.

In the North Sea, APA has suspended all new drilling activity and will cease all production by the end of 2029. As of recently, the company continued its economic assessment of its North Sea assets in light of several new regulatory guidelines and obligations surrounding significant tax levies and the modernization of aging infrastructure. APA determined that potential returns do not economically support making investments required under the current regulations.

Suriname has become an incredible opportunity for APA. Back in October 2024, APA announced that it reached a positive final investment decision for the first oil development. The fields are estimated to have recoverable resources of over 750 million barrels of oil and will feature an FPSO unit with a capacity of 220,000 BOE/d. APA is rightfully quite euphoric about GranMorgu, as the company's interest in GranMorgu would equate to 400 Midland Basin locations at 30% of the cost. The total investment is estimated at $10.5 billion, which will be split between Total and APA. The first oil is anticipated in 2028, and here's how the company forecasts the economics of the field:

APA also has two offshore exploration blocks in Uruguay, situated in the Pelotas Basin. It currently plans to drill exploration wells in the area. Furthermore, the company set up a JV in Alaska that holds around 326,000 gross acres with a high-quality oil discovery at King Street #1.

Valuation

APA has some of the lowest valuation metrics out of the O&G sector, as the company currently has an A- valuation grade from Seeking Alpha. It currently has a P/E Non-GAAP of 4.02, 60.37% lower than the sector median, which is already depressed amid current market conditions. EV/EBITDA also stands at a dismal 2.19 while P/CF is currently standing at 1.51. FCF yield is fairly high at around 14%, assuming a stock price of $15.04.

However, the low valuation is also due to lower margins compared to peers, as the company has faced broad costs due to its international set of assets. It incurs higher costs outside of the O&G-friendly Texas, such as, for example, its Egypt operation that has $10-12/BOE LOE compared to Magnolia's $4/BOE Eagle Ford efficiency. It also incurred impairments in the North Sea worth $571 million, plus the massive investments in GranMorgu that affect its bottom line. The company also faced integration costs with Callon Petroleum, further straining margins. However, I'll say that APA's LFCF margin is fairly strong, and so are the company's return metrics.

Based on the company's strength of generating FCF, APA has a strong capital return policy that further polishes the stock:

  • Currently, the E&P company has instated a 60/40 Capital Return Framework where 60% of FCF is returned to shareholders via dividends and buybacks.
  • The company has a strong dividend yield of 6.65%, which has grown by 61.48% over the last 3 years CAGR. The yield is relatively safe at a dividend payout ratio of 26.74%.
  • APA has also implemented a substantial share repurchase program in recent years, totaling $2.745 billion since 2021. That was a 20.14% total share outstanding reduction up to Dec '23. However, following the Callon Petroleum acquisition, the total share count increased back to 364.1 million.

Another headline-grabbing number would be the company's debt level. APA has a total debt of $6.417B, 105% of the total equity in the business. That's huge; however, the company has been upgraded from "BB+" to "BBB-", lowering potential interest rates on future debt raising. Furthermore, the company plans to decrease this debt by $2 billion over the next three years. This has been proven by both the $950 million and $750 million previous divestments, and the strong FCF generation, which is easily covering the company's current interest coverage ratio.

Risks

At the end of the day, APA is reliant on oil and natural gas prices. Breakeven oil price was reduced to $61 per barrel, thanks to the Callon integration, down from $78 in 2023. APA's Suriname assets are expected to have breakeven levels below $20 per barrel, which could be tremendous for APA's flexibility in down markets and its FCF generation abilities. This is all good; however, the market has been crashing since the implementation of the current administration's tariffs and the ongoing U.S.-China trade war. If prices go even lower, this would seriously hurt APA and its expansion plans, especially considering the company's debt level.

Conclusion

I believe that APA is a buy at current prices with its significantly low valuation. It is risky, especially with falling WTI prices, but the Suriname prospects and the current Permian operation make the company worthwhile for any investors looking to buy into a broad asset base E&P.

data.title: Q2 Holdings: Bull Case Centers On Margin Expansion And Land-And-Expand Strength
data.description:
I covered Q2 Holdings (NYSE:QTWO) on one prior occasion:

  • Q2 Holdings: Expanding Margins And Tangible Momentum Make It A Buy (October 2024)

At the time, Q2’s shares were trading for approximately $80. As part of my analysis, I highlighted Q2’s expanding margins, tangible momentum, and fair valuation as reasons to justify a ‘Buy’ rating. In the couple of months following the publication, Q2’s stock rose significantly. In December, shares were trading for over $110. However, since the start of 2025, shares have slipped. Today, they are trading for around $75.

For a comprehensive overview of Q2’s business model, historical financial performance, and notable risks, refer to my previous analysis. In this analysis, I will be reviewing their Q4 2024 earnings report and full-year financial results. In the end, I will reiterate my ‘Buy’ rating for Q2’s stock.

Financials

Q2 finished 2024 with $696.5 million of total revenue. This represents 11.5% year-over-year growth from the $624.6 million of revenue they generated in 2023. While this is modest top-line growth, it’s worth noting that Q2’s subscription ARR, which also happens to be the highest quality form of revenue, grew 15% year-over-year. So although overall revenue leaves something to be desired, it’s reassuring that subscription ARR growth is outpacing the pie as a whole.

From their $696.5 million of revenue, Q2 generated $354.5 million of gross profit. Their gross margin for the year checked in at 50.9%. Additionally, their Q4 gross margin was 52.6%—indicating that the company’s gross margins continue to expand. This trend of margin expansion continues onto the operating side as well. For the full year 2024, Q2 generated a $42.3 million operating loss. However, the operating loss in Q4 alone was only $1.6 million.

Despite their operating loss in Q4, the company posted their first-ever net profit as a public company. Net income in Q4 was $0.2 million. Although a few hundred thousand dollars of net income is akin to a rounding error for a company generating close to $700 million of revenue, it’s a notable step forward for Q2 as they transition to consistent GAAP profitability.

Q2 continues to perform well on a cash basis. The company generated $135.8 million of cash from operations in 2024, while spending only $6.7 million on capex. As I noted in my previous analysis, a significant portion of Q2’s uplift from net income to free cash flow comes from adding back stock-based compensation. Total stock-based compensation in 2024 was $89.2 million. While I wouldn’t consider this a concerning amount, it is a trend worth monitoring in the future—particularly as the company transitions to profitability.

This healthy cash generation helped Q2 add $127.2 million of cash to their balance sheet over the course of the year. Currently, the company is sitting on $446.6 million of total cash and short-term investments.

Overall, Q2 posted a strong financial performance in 2024. Their subscription ARR grew in the mid-teens, gross and operating margins expanded, and they posted their first quarter of GAAP profitability. Moreover, the fundamentals of their business model remain strong as they continue to generate large sums of cash.

Land-and-Expand

A key takeaway from Q2’s earnings is its successful land and expand strategy. The company highlighted its 57% average customer contracted revenue growth at 48 months. In other words, customers who have been with Q2 for four years are contributing 57% more revenue to Q2 than they were when they first signed on.

As the above graph shows, contract expansion is modest in the first year, before it jumps up in the second, third, and fourth years. This demonstrates that Q2 is able to consistently cross-sell and deliver value for their customers.

Why does this matter? Two things: net revenue retention and operating leverage. Although Q2 doesn’t report specifics for revenue retention, it can be assumed that they have healthy net revenue retention based on their strong expansion rates. And the importance of high net revenue retention is critical, as it’s often a key driver of valuations for software companies. Secondly, customer expansion is one of the highest-margin ways to grow revenue. Instead of having to spend on sales and marketing to land brand-new customers, cross-selling to their existing customers allows each dollar to go further.

Valuation

This begs the question, what is Q2 worth? In my previous analysis, I compared its valuation to that of Temenos AG (OTCPK:TMSNY), another fintech company developing software solutions for banks and other financial institutions.

Today, Q2 is trading for a trailing EV/Revenue multiple of 6.7x, while Temenos trades for 5.7x. On a forward basis, Q2 trades at 6x sales, and Temenos goes for 5.6x.

So, does Q2 merit the marginally higher multiple? I’d argue yes. After all, Q2’s top-line growth is almost triple that of Temenos’. On top of that, Q2’s five-year revenue CAGR is 17.2%, which far outpaces Temenos’ 1.4%.

Looking forward, Q2 wants to replicate their best parts of 2024. They hope to grow subscription ARR ~15% year-over-year, expand adjusted EBITDA margins by 360 basis points each year, and post an 85%+ free cash flow conversion by 2026. Each of these goals seems reasonable and achievable. If they are able to execute this plan successfully, I wouldn’t be surprised to see them trading for 7.5x-10x. Buying in now creates an opportunity to hold on for multiple expansion—fueled by the underlying margin expansion.

Risks

We live in a world of rapidly advancing technology. It has never been easier to develop software than it is today. And for many application software companies, this is an ominous reality. After all, the easier software development becomes, the more likely it is that companies will opt to ditch their ballooning SaaS bills, and opt to develop software in house. Over the long term, only the companies that have the highest retention, stickiest products, and strongest brands will remain.

While this headwind is certainly a reality for Q2, I believe that they are well-positioned to combat it. Q2 creates software solutions for consumer banking. At the end of the day, the last thing that a bank wants to happen is to bring their software development in-house and end up creating a solution that has a poor user experience. Even worse, they could run the risk of a security breach. For this reason, I believe that a product like Q2’s will be far more sticky than something like an internal-only tool for enterprises.

Regardless, following the aggregate valuation trends for software companies will be key. While macro software headwinds continue, companies like Q2 could be positioned to outperform in an environment with compressed valuations.

Conclusion

Q2 is transitioning to profitability at an optimal time. The market has been volatile over the last few months, and companies that are yet to reach profitability will continue to be scrutinized by investors. Q2 has the chance to stand out from the rest as they boast accelerating margin expansion, strong cash generation, and increased financial discipline.

Q2’s land-and-expand strategy will continue to be key in driving sizable margin expansion. In addition, successful customer expansion reinforces investor conviction for their ability to retain revenue. As margins expand and sentiment regarding revenue retention remains strong, valuation multiples would be primed to expand. Couple this with their reasonable valuation relative to peers, and Q2 offers a compelling bull case amidst a noisy macro environment for software companies. As such, I am reiterating my ‘Buy’ rating for Q2.

data.title: BGY: Underlying Fundamentals Can Be Improved
data.description:
Overview

BlackRock Enhanced International Dividend Trust (NYSE:BGY) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide an attractive total return from its diverse portfolio of equities. What makes BGY unique is the global exposure that the fund provides, making it a good choice for investors looking to diversify their portfolio away from US markets. The fund has an inception dating back to 2007 and has a management fee of only 1%.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, BGY's price has only slightly declined by about 0.2%, with most of that decline happening over the last two weeks. This can be attributed to the recent market decline caused by uncertainty around President Trump's tariff proposals. When including all the distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 7.4% over the same period. BGY has a generous starting dividend yield of about 9.8%. Additionally, distributions are paid out on a monthly basis, which makes the fund attractive for income seeking investors.

The latest fund reporting indicates a mixed history of earnings, which has delayed NAV growth over time. Additionally, the fund has a history of reducing distributions over time, bringing into question whether or not BGY makes a good long-term holding. Despite the unique makeup of the fund, BGY has traditionally underperformed its own holdings over time.

Fund Strategy

According to BGY's latest overview, the fund has net assets totaling over $537M that is spread across 234 different holdings. While BGY does have some small exposure to US equities, it only makes up a small percentage of assets. The fund has varying weights across regions of Europe, Asia, and North America.

  • United Kingdom: 15.12%
  • United States: 11.23%
  • France: 11.21%
  • Germany: 10.40%
  • Japan: 8.41%
  • Switzerland: 5.96%
  • Taiwan: 5.71%
  • Netherlands: 4.89%
  • Canada: 4.40%
  • Sweden: 4.33%

From a sector perspective, investments in Financial companies make up the largest weight at 19.99%. This is followed by weight to Industrial and Information Technology companies, accounting for 18.22% and 16.25% of assets respectively. However, the combination of these three sectors makes up over 54% of total assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on BGY's daily price changes and total return potential.

The fund primarily invests in large-cap companies and chooses to allocate 80% of its assets in non-US dividend paying companies. As a result, the top holding is currently Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) at 5.85% of assets. This is followed by a 4.10% weight in Novo Nordisk (NVO) and a 3.67% weight in AstraZeneca (AZN). Since the fund doesn't deploy any sort of leverage as part of its strategy, BGY relies on the combination of dividend income and capital gains from these positions to generate its earnings.

To help amplify earnings, BGY does deploy an option strategy to generate additional income from its holdings. BGY implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategy so that the fund can partake in some of the capital appreciation of the holdings. OTM option strategies involve selecting a strike price that is typically between 5% to 15% above the current market price of the underlying asset. For example, if BGY holds an asset priced at $100 per share, the fund may select a strike price of $110 per share.

Risk Profile

The main risk of BGY is that the fund will underperform its own holdings. The inclusion of an option strategy naturally limits the capital appreciation to the selected strike price. So even if international markets experience a strong bullish movement upwards, BGY will only partially partake in this. Conversely, an investor is still exposed to the full downside risk. So if international markets saw declines, BGY would fully partake in the downward trend. This is why BGY will continue to underperform its own holdings over a longer period of time. So unless you are an investor who needs to put emphasis on income generation, I wouldn't recommend maintaining a position in BGY.

Since the fund only maintains a small exposure to the US markets, there should be no expectation for BGY to track traditional indices. US markets frequently outperform international markets, and this trend can continue over time if the growth of AI starts to materialize and lead to higher valuations. BGY is likely to underperform if the size of the AI market continues to increase.

Additionally, BGY has underperformed more traditional international dividend ETFs despite having a much higher starting dividend yield. For instance, BGY has lower total returns than Vanguard International High Dividend Yield Index Fund (VYMI) and Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY), which can also be attributed to the inclusion of the option strategy.

Valuation

Since BGY operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, BGY currently trades at a discount to NAV of 10.2%. For reference, BGY has traded at an average discount to NAV of 11.87% over the last three years. The fact that BGY consistently trades at a discounted valuation could be a direct reflection of what the market thinks about the fund. BGY hasn't demonstrated any strong NAV growth in the past, and earnings have remained inconsistent.

The most recent annual report is dated as of December 2024 and reveals some underlying flaws. For instance, net investment income only amounted to $0.09 per share while net realized gains were $0.02 per share for 2024, which is extremely weak. Looking back over prior years, net investment income levels have always been weak and hasn't surpassed $0.12 per share since 2020.

I find this a bit odd considering that the fund deploys an option strategy and has exposure to dividend paying companies. Net realized gains seem to be highly dependent on positive market momentum, which can be a source of weakness during market downturns. As a result, BGY's NAV has been inconsistent over recent years. Here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $6.49 per share
  • 2021: $6.81 per share
  • 2022: $5.78 per share
  • 2023: $6.21 per share
  • 2024: $5.91 per share

The poor net investment income levels ultimately leave BGY reliant on net realized gains. As you can imagine, it can be difficult for a fund to achieve positive net realized gains during periods where markets are in decline. I think the fund can definitely improve if net investment income levels were higher because it may create a more stable price and better resilient during market downturns. The issue is that BGY still puts emphasis on dividends, even when the fund may not have actually generated enough earnings to fully support it. This can also exacerbate the issue of a stagnant NAV over time.

However, BGY can be a good choice to offset current market volatility. Since the holdings are primarily focused outside of the US, we can see how BGY outperformed traditional index-tracking ETFs like SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) or Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) on a YTD basis. BGY has been able to better navigate the market declines caused by President Trump's tariffs.

Questionable Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.0426 per share, the current dividend yield sits at 9.8%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.5112 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, BGY has only been able to generate earnings in excess of this amount for three of the last five years. This doesn't exactly instill a lot of confidence that BGY will be able to sustain the higher payout following the recent dividend raise to start 2025. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: $0.43 per share
  • 2021: $0.73 per share
  • 2022: ($0.62) per share
  • 2023: $0.84 per share
  • 2024: $0.11 per share

So despite the monthly distributions and higher starting yield, it's a bit difficult to recommend BGY for long-term investors. Looking over the past decade, we can see several instances where the distributions were reduced in the past. If a fund has a history of reducing payouts, it sort of defeats the purpose of maintaining a position if the dividend is always under threat of a reduction. For this reason, I cannot suggest BGY as a suitable long-term holding.

Just to demonstrate the weakness, I ran a back test of a $25,000 investment at the start of 2016. The graph below assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $25k, and also assumes that no dividends were reinvested back into the fund. As we can see, the annual income amounts have gradually declined over time.

2016 Dividend Income: $2,091

2024 Dividend Income: $1,660

Historically, most of the distributions have been funded by a combination of net investment income and net realized gains. Therefore, investors should expect that portions of their distributions will be classified as ordinary dividend income, which has less favorable tax consequences. Based on your specific circumstance, a position in BGY may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account. However, BGY has also implemented return of capital distributions in the past. These distributions aren't directly taxed, but instead reduce an investor's cost basis, which defers taxes until the time of sale.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate BGY as a hold due to the inconsistent NAV growth, mixed underlying performance, and weak distribution history. While BGY is a unique fund that can provide some global exposure, the fund's fundamentals can be improved. Net investment income has historically remained very weak while net realized gains have been inconsistent over time. The fund also has an inconsistent distribution coverage history over time, bringing into question whether or not BGY can be a good choice for long-term investors. Despite the price trading at a discount to NAV, I don't necessarily believe this to be an opportunity at the moment.

data.title: VPU: Utilities Poised To Generate Lofty Returns In 2025 And Beyond
data.description:
Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VPU) has been outperforming the S&P 500 as robust demand is bolstering investment activities in the sector. Companies seek to invest aggressively in growth opportunities to capitalize on growing demand, as EVs along with AI-powered data centers are likely to increase consumption year over year in the next decade. Therefore, it appears to be a prime time to invest in the utilities sector to capitalize on explosive growth trends in the years to come. Investment options, such as Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares, enable investors to gain access to the broader utilities sector through a single investment. Therefore, I initiate coverage of VPU with a buy rating.

The Utilities Sector is Poised to Grow in 2025 and Beyond

After years of stagnant growth, the utilities sector is outperforming the S&P 500. The outperformance is attributed to the robust demand outlook. The demand from AI-powered data centers, which is expected to increase in the next five years at a compound annual rate of 15%, is likely to be the biggest contributor to consumption growth. Whereas, the US seeks to completely shift to EVs over the long term, with a plan to make 50% of new vehicles zero-emission by 2030. The shift to EVs is expected to increase electricity consumption.

According to the EIA estimate, the US electricity demand is likely to reach all-time highs of 4,201 billion kilowatt-hours and 4,244 billion kilowatt-hours in 2025 and 2026. The US consumed a record 4,082 kWh in 2024. Whereas, concerns over the electricity shortfall are growing. John Moura, NERC’s director of reliability assessments and planning analysis, predicts that half of the US is at a risk of blackouts. He anticipates that summer demand could be 15% higher in the next decade compared to the current peak.

Companies in the utilities sector have also been increasing their capital investment plans to capitalize on explosive growth trends. For example, NextEra (NEE) plans to double its $20 billion investment in Texas while The Southern Company (SO) forecasts a base capital investment of $63 billion in the next five years. Duke Energy (DUK) and American Electric Power (AEP) announced capital investment plans of $83 billion and $63 billion to capitalize on the robust demand. Dominion Energy (D) recently hiked its five-year capital investment forecast by 16% to $50 billion. Other large companies such as Sempra (SRE), Exelon (EXC) and Vistra (VST) also seek to aggressively invest in growth opportunities to capitalize on demand.

How Does VPU is An Attractive Investment Vehicle?

Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares generated the strong price returns for its shareholders in the past twelve months, beating the S&P 500 by more than 17%. As the utilities sector offers a higher dividend yield, VPU’s total returns hover around 24% in the past twelve months. The ETF is also trading in the green year to date, compared to hefty losses by the broader stock market index.

VPU’s outperformance reflects explosive growth trends in the utilities sector. In addition, the fund’s performance is also attributed to its diversified portfolio structure, enabling it to capitalize on the broader trends in the utilities sector. Although a single stock investment in the fast-growing company can increase the chance for a higher share price appreciation, the risk of a loss is also greater in the case of any uncertain event.

Whereas, in the case of VPU, its portfolio is composed of 70 stocks, with the top 10 accounting for half of the portfolio weight. Consequently, the underperformance of any stock or a group of few stocks have a limited impact on its overall portfolio performance. VPU’s top 10 holdings comprises popular names, including NextEra, The Southern Company, Duke Energy, American Electric Power, Constellation Energy (CEG), Dominion Energy, Sempra, Exelon, Vistra and Public Service Enterprise (PEG).

The fund also earned a buy rating with a quant score of 4.31. It received the strong grades on all five quant factors. An A-plus grade on a momentum factor is attributed to its strong share price performance compared to the median of all ETFs. Technically, ETFs with a strong momentum are expected to sustain their uptrend.

Moreover, its dividend yield of more than 3% and a solid dividend growth rate enabled it to achieve a high grade on a dividend factor. Its dividend growth rate in the trailing twelve months was 7%. Companies in the utilities sector are expected to increase their dividend growth rate amid expectations for a high single digit revenue and earnings growth forecast in the next five years. VPU’s expense ratio of 0.09% is significantly lower than the median of all ETFs. The fund has a healthy average daily shares trading volume of 219K and assets under management of $8.50 billion.

Risk Factors to Consider

Although the utilities sector is less-cyclical in nature, it can still struggle in the case of a recession and slower economic growth over the long term. This is because a slower economic growth can impact electricity consumption from residential, business and industrial consumers. A slower economic growth can also decline data center and electric vehicle demand. Furthermore, the ETF is also exposed to tail events.

In Conclusion

As the utilities sector is in its early stage of explosive growth trend in the next decade, I believe it's a perfect time to initiate a long-term position through investment vehicles like Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares. It is a passively managed ETF with a diversified portfolio structure and low expense ratio. Furthermore, despite a recent share price appreciation, the ETF appears cheap with 20x trailing and 17x forward earnings.

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment return and share price will fluctuate with market conditions, and investors may have a gain or loss when shares are sold. Mutual Fund performance changes over time and currently may be significantly lower than stated above. Performance is updated and published monthly. Current month-end performance can be obtained atdodgeandcox.com or by calling 800-621-3979.

Market Commentary

The S&P 500 fell 4.3% in the first quarter of 2025, resulting in the worst quarterly performance for U.S. equities in more than two years. Market volatility and downside pressure increased near the end of the quarter, in reaction to policy shifts and uncertainty around increased tariffs. Recent economic data indicated a slowing economy and weakening consumer confidence.

The first quarter saw a reversal in the performance of the “Magnificent Seven” with each of these large companies posting negative returns after their market-leading performance over the last two years. U.S. value stocks outperformed growth stocks in the first quarter, marking a substantial reversal from 2024. 2 While the valuation disparity between value and growth stocks narrowed, the Russell 1000 Value traded at 17.2 times forward earnings, a meaningful discount to the Russell 1000 Growth Index at 25.6 times forward earnings on March 31. 3

Portfolio Strategy

In the current uncertain economic climate, we are closely reviewing risks and opportunities and continue to take counter-cyclical actions to identify undervalued investments, consistent with our approach as active, value-oriented investors.

In early 2025, we added to holdings in the Industrials, Materials, Real Estate, and Health Care sectors, and trimmed select holdings in Financials, continuing a similar trend from last year. In 2024, the Health Care sector faced significant challenges due to margin pressures and concerns about the potential for adverse regulatory changes. After being 2024's largest detractor, Health Care was the top-contributing sector to the Fund’s relative performance during the first quarter of 2025. Our activity in the shares of CVS Health is an example of our contrarian, long-term approach. 4

CVS has rebounded strongly after weak 2024 performance, up over 50% in the first quarter. 2024 was a difficult year for CVS due to weaker sales at its pharmacies and higher medical costs in its Medicare Advantage health insurance segment. The company’s results rebounded in the fourth quarter under new CEO David Joyner, who joined in October. The strong results fueled investor hopes for a turnaround. Consistent with our contrarian approach, we added to CVS during 2024 and early 2025 to take advantage of the company’s depressed valuation and our positive long-term outlook for the company.

Recent Market Volatility

The Trump administration's tariff policy rollout in the first weeks of April increased fears of higher inflation and a possible recession, which led to increased market volatility. When market prices change, we revisit our investment theses security-by-security, incorporate recent developments, reassess our long-term outlook, and make incremental changes to the portfolio. We’ve navigated challenging economic periods in the past and have used them to uncover attractive long-term investment opportunities at compelling valuations. Our past experiences give us confidence in our investment approach and ability to serve our shareholders' long-term best interests through the market’s current turbulence.

We remain optimistic about the long-term prospects for the Fund, which trades at a significant discount to the broader market and is diversified across sectors and investment themes. On March 31, the Fund traded at only 14.1 times forward earnings, compared to 20.9 times for the S&P 500 and 17.2 times for the Russell 1000 Value.

We encourage shareholders to take a long-term view and are here to answer any questions you may have.

Thank you for the continued confidence you’ve placed in Dodge & Cox.

Performance Review (Fund’s Class I Shares vs. S&P 500) First Quarter

Key contributors to relative results included the Fund's:

  • Overweight position and holdings in Health Care, particularly CVS Health;
  • Information Technology underweight position and stock selection;
  • and Position in RTX.

Key detractors from relative results included the Fund's:

  • Positions in Avantor (AVTR) and FedEx (FDX).

Performance Review (Fund’s Class I Shares vs. Russell 1000 Value) First Quarter

Key contributors to relative results included the Fund's:

  • Stock selection and overweight position in Health Care, including CVS Health, Sanofi, and Gilead Sciences (GILD);
  • Industrials stock selection, notably RTX; and
  • Position in Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD)(OTCPK:BUDFF).

Key detractors from relative results included the Fund's:

  • Communication Services holdings, mainly Alphabet; and
  • Positions in Amazon, FedEx, and Avantor.

data.title: BKCH: Lagging Peers And Overly Concentrated
data.description:
The Global X Blockchain ETF (NASDAQ:BKCH) is an actively managed fund that offers investors exposure to companies that stand to benefit from blockchain technology innovation. Some of the ways the fund builds that exposure is through publicly listed equities that touch blockchain technology either through digital asset transactions, cryptocurrency mining, mining hardware, or integration with blockchain networks. In my initial coverage of BKCH, we'll look at fund details compared to peers, holdings, and whether I personally think the ticker is one to consider for investment.

BKCH Details

There are really only a handful of ETFs that I have come across that have attempted to build a 'blockchain' fund as a theme. While this isn't an exhaustive list, I see the closest natural peers for BKCH as the Fidelity Crypto Industry and Digital Payments ETF (FDIG), the VanEck Digital Transformation ETF (DAPP), and the iShares Blockchain and Tech ETF (IBLC). The latter of which is the smallest at just $27.3 million in AUM.

Blockchain ETFs BKCH FDIG DAPP IBLC
Issuer Global X Funds Fidelity Investments VanEck iShares
Inception 07/12/2021 04/19/2022 04/12/2021 04/25/2022
Expense Ratio 0.50% 0.40% 0.51% 0.47%
AUM $113,160,512 $132,467,749 $133,702,510 $27,296,737

Source: Seeking Alpha, AUM as of 4/15/25

Fee-wise, the funds are all very close though FDIG is cheapest. But at 50 basis points, I don't think BKCH is terribly overpriced for an actively managed fund. However, the fund is very concentrated in top stocks and only has about two dozen positions.

Blockchain ETFs BKCH FDIG DAPP IBLC
Number of Holdings 25 53 22 48
Assets in Top 10 74.8% 68.5% 62.9% 66.4%
Turnover 56.4% 53.0% 57.0% 81.0%

Source: Seeking Alpha, as of 4/15/25

The Global X Blockchain ETF has a similar turnover ratio to FDIG and DAPP at a little over 56% and has less holdings than FDIG at just 25. Perhaps the biggest thing that separates BKCH from the rest of the pack is the concentration in the fund's top 10 holdings. At just under 75% of assets in the top 10 holdings, this is a fund that I'd call reliant on its top stocks.

ETF Grades BKCH FDIG DAPP IBLC
Momentum F D- F F
Expenses C+ B- C+ C+
Dividends A- F D- C+
Risk F F F F
Liquidity C C+ B F

Source: Seeking Alpha, as of 4/15/25

One thing that is interesting is the fund gets an 'A+' Dividend grade from Seeking Alpha while the other funds are well below that. At 11.5%, BKCH has a very high trailing twelve month yield. The TTM yield on DAPP is 6.3% and IBLC paid out 2.2% over the last year.

Performance vs The Peer Group

Since three of these funds are actively paying dividends, it's important to look at the performance of the funds through the total return rather than just the share price performance. From a total return standpoint, these ETFs have all had generally awful starts to 2025:

At a 34% negative total return, BKCH is closer to the worst performer of the peer group shown above. The 1-year performance is also quite poor with BKCH standing out as the clear laggard of the group:

With all of these funds generally sharing similar themes and 12 month trends, it ultimately comes down to where the major allocations are to discern what might explain the underperformance of BKCH relative to the other funds.

Holdings Breakdown

Before we get into the deeper BKCH breakdown, I wanted to look at the top 3 assets for each fund as well as the allocation to those top three positions. For ease of reading the table, I'm keeping it to just the tickers. The unique stocks that make it into these top 3 positions are the following:

  • Coinbase Global (COIN)
  • MARA Holdings (MARA)
  • Riot Platforms (RIOT)
  • CleanSpark (CLSK)
  • Strategy (MSTR)
  • Block, Inc (XYZ)

Unsurprisingly, there are a lot crypto-focused companies and Bitcoin (BTC-USD) mining stocks. Three of the funds have top equity exposure to Coinbase:

Top 3 Stocks BKCH FDIG DAPP IBLC
1st COIN COIN MSTR COIN
2nd MARA MARA COIN MARA
3rd RIOT CLSK XYZ RIOT
Top 3 Weighting 37.0% 25.0% 25.4% 30.8%

Source: Seeking Alpha, as of 4/15/25

I should note that this table puts COIN as FDIG's top position, but FDIG actually has 23.8% of AUM in the income-based Fidelity Securities Lending Cash Central Fund. Which could possibly disqualify that fund from consideration entirely since it might not make sense to pay Fidelity 40 basis points to put a quarter of the fund's assets in T-bills. The flip side of that coin is perhaps FDIG is better insulated from drawdowns - and we can certainly see that reflected in FDIG's relative outperformance year to date.

Here's the full asset breakdown for BKCH specifically:

57.4% of the fund is allocated to Bitcoin mining stocks. To me, that seems like a large allocation. For an actively managed fund, I think there can/should be more discernment regarding what is included in a 'blockchain' ETF. In my opinion, there are some fairly notable omissions from this fund that I would think would fit with the overall thesis of businesses using blockchain. Some examples of publicly traded companies that I'm surprised didn't make it in the fund would be Exodus Movement, Inc. (NYSE:EXOD), Fold Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:FLD), and Coincheck Group N.V. (NASDAQ:CNCK). Furthermore, Ethereum (ETH-USD) mining with GPUs produced by NVIDIA (NVDA) ended over 2.5 years ago. That BKCH has almost as much exposure to NVDA as it does to a company like Bit Digital (BTBT) seems off base for a 'blockchain' fund.

Closing Thoughts

I think investors who want exposure to 'blockchain' as an idea can probably do better picking their own stocks. Beyond direct buys of Coinbase, MARA Holdings, and maybe Galaxy Digital (GLXY:CA)(OTCPK:BRPHF), there are a lot of companies in this fund that investors can likely pass on if they want to play the 'blockchain' ponies. Even something like EXOD, which I'm not actually bullish, makes more thematic sense than some of the other companies included in BKCH. At the end of the day, it's a fund with a lot of capital allocated to a handful of crypto-adjacent stocks and a high reliance on a mining sector that faces long term economic challenges. For me, BKCH is a 'hold.'

data.title: Live Oak Bancshares: Bank On It Today
data.description:
Amid macroeconomic uncertainty in the US, banks may face some challenges in their profitability and sustainability. Yet, some exude confidence with their continued expansion, strategic business focus, and prudent asset management. One of them is Live Oak Bancshares, Inc. (NYSE:LOB). It was established at the height of the Great Recession, which shows that breakthroughs could be achieved even in the most challenging situations. With its stable topline performance and sound Balance Sheet, I believe the bank can still do well amid some headwinds. In addition, it’s cheap now and deserves to bounce back.

LOB Q4 2024: A Decent Year-End

Live Oak Bancshares, Inc. ended the year with a solid performance amid its continued expansion and increased exposure to SMBs.

Its interest income amounted to $212.6M, up by 14.0% YoY From $186.5M. We can note that growth has become somewhat toppish in the past six quarters. It just became flatter and more obvious during the second half of the year.

If we look at interest income on loans, it rose by 14.9% from Q4 2023. However, its difference from Q3 2024 was only 1.6%. The same can be seen in investment income since these are mostly bonds and mortgage-backed securities. We can attribute it to the sharp rate cut by the Fed during H2 2024. From August to September, it stashed it by 100 bps.

Nonetheless, LOB saw a stronger net interest income before provisions as it increased by 18.7% YoY and 2.8% QoQ to $115.1M. After all, interest rate cuts also led to lower interest expense on deposits and borrowings.

Considering all these aspects, I do believe LOB remains resilient amid the offsetting effect of the policy easing cycle on its interest income and expense. It is also worth noting that LOB gained more consumers’ and borrowers’ confidence, as shown by its increasing loan and deposit base. The fact that LOB sustained growth despite higher credit loss provisions shows that expansion stays fruitful. This can also show prudent management of its interest-sensitive assets and liabilities. I will discuss this more later.

Laggards

LOB is well-positioned today, but some risks are already on the horizon. Trade war poses the biggest threat to its performance as it can spur inflation and volatility in the long run. This can also make it hard for the Fed to continue its policy easing cycle or at least stabilize inflation and interest rates. This can affect the performance of LOB since macroeconomic uncertainties can lower confidence among its clients and borrowers. Loan repayment capacity and deposit base may also be affected, so it must be cautious.

Despite this, I hope that the falling Brent and WTI prices can mitigate these threats. The price drop can be supported by the OPEC oil supply increase in the next two months. If the trend continues, it can partially offset the impact of tariffs through lower fuel, transportation, and shipping expenses. This can help industries heavily affected by oil prices, such as airlines, public transportation, shipping, automobiles, restaurants, and freight and logistics. Hence, companies can absorb the impact of tariffs or lower the amount to be passed on to consumers to stabilize economic activities.

Drivers

Despite the potential risks, I think LOB will remain stable with these attributes.

Well-Positioned Loan/Deposit Base For Market Uncertainties

After the uptick to 3.00% last January, inflation slowed again to 2.8% last February and 2.4% last March. With tariff concerns and oil price drops, the Fed may keep the interest rate unchanged. I think this will provide Live Oak some breathing room to reprice loans and deposits and stabilize yields.

It has not yet hinted at the portion of loans for repricing. But we must note that 88% of its loans have variable rates. And I think these are well-positioned if we consider how LOB diversifies them. Its portfolio is concentrated on commercial loans, which are typically more secure. This makes LOB more shielded.

It may be confusing at first knowing that net charge offs rose and affected its net interest income. Yet, we must remember that its loan base rose by 24.6% in only a year. As we can see, it made a substantial but reasonable increase in loan allowances to account for delinquencies and defaults. And I think this is a conservative approach of the company despite its continued expansion.

Meanwhile, 3% of its deposits are non-interest-bearing. But half of its interest-bearing deposits are time deposits, which are typically fixed deposits. These can give LOB some flexibility to potential Fed rate changes. So even if uncertainty arises amid tariff and recession woes, this portion of deposits will remain unaffected. It is also important to note that only 2.2% of its deposits are demand deposits. This lowers deposit run risks.

Lastly, it keeps its loan-to-deposit ratio manageable at 87.0%, which is still within the ideal range of 80-90%. It still has adequate reserves to cover delinquencies. This proves LOB maintains a conservative approach to growth in the current market condition.

Well-Diversified Loan Portfolio

Aside from loan rates, I like its concentration on commercial loans. A substantial part of it is purely commercial banking for small businesses. It’s a prudent focus considering that SMBs comprise 99% of US businesses. This makes it easy for LOB to increase its loan origination and expand its customer base. The idea that a substantial portion of its loans and deposits have variable rates offers flexibility to borrowers and depositors.

In addition, its real estate exposure is also purely commercial. CRE loans comprise 39.5% of its total loans. Since these are mostly small businesses, LOB is geared more towards retail spaces. As we can see, retail properties have the most stable supply and demand. This means more stable property prices and mortgages. This also has the steadiest asset value and returns, making them desirable amid macroeconomic volatility. This is probably the reason its loan yields continue to increase as it expands despite the 1% rate cut in only a few months.

Another thing I like about commercial loans is that they are less interest-sensitive, so they are more stable. If the rate cut continues, the decrease in loan yields will be manageable. If the economy becomes more volatile, they can be handled well. After all, most of them have variable rates, so LOB can adjust them to market conditions.

Lastly, 31.6% of its loans are guaranteed. This means that the specific portion of its loans is backed by third parties, mainly the US Small Business Administration or the US SBA. This limits the risk of insolvency and gives it an extra capital buffer.

Well-Capitalized Portfolio

LOB has solid capital adequacy with an 11.0% CET1 Ratio. It means that LOB’s high-quality capital will cover 11.0% of its risk-weighted assets. Its Tier 1 Capital Ratio is the same, showing that all its capital is high quality. If we add the other capital, the Total Capital Ratio will increase to 12.3%. This can prove LOB’s adequate capacity to absorb potential losses without getting insolvent. Note that this is about 1.5x the minimum ratio of 8.0% required by Basel III. Hence, LOB can withstand a financial crisis or a recession even without resorting to borrowing. It also proves that LOB has enough capital buffer to prevent or at least minimize deposit run risks.

Price Ratios

At this point, we are certain that LOB has excellent fundamentals, positioning it well against macroeconomic volatility. So, we will also know whether LOB is worth the risk for its current valuation.

As a bank, a reliable metric to use is the P/B Ratio. However, I noticed that over the years, the price had not been too sensitive or responsive to it. If we trace back its movement, it was more consistent with the P/E Ratio. Before I assess the stock, I decided to analyze the price versus the EPS and BVPS using a regression analysis.

As you can see, there is an 86% correlation, showing their direct relationship. But using the p-value, the relationship between the EPS and the stock price is the significant one. It is only 0.0169, which means that only 1.69% of their relationship happened by chance. This can erase the randomness and bias of our observation. Meanwhile, the p-value of the BVPS is high at 0.268. This means that 26.8% of their data are random.

Despite this, I decided to use them both to compare the value of the stock relative to the previous years. They both show that LOB is much cheaper today than the previous years. If we focus on the P/E, LOB is trading at 13.9x its EPS, which is lower than the five-year average of 17.1x. This gives a TP of $28.83, a 22.7% upside potential. Likewise, the P/B shows underpricing. While the TP appears too much, the actual P/B Ratio today at 1.07x is more reasonable than the previous years.

Although these are historical numbers, we can get a clue about the price direction relative to these metrics, especially the EPS. The agreement between the statistical analysis and the P/E Ratio confirms its undervaluation.

Technicals

LOB has shown a weak momentum recently as both the price and the 50-day SMA fell below the 200-day SMA. Despite this, the flat 200-day SMA shows stability in its long-term trend. This also shows that the downtrend is due to negative sentiments recently. Technical indicators do not suggest an instant rebound as MACD remains in the negative region.

However, we can find buying opportunities at the current price for several reasons. First, the stock is already trading at its 52-week low, confirming its cheapness. Second, MACD’s histogram is still in the negative region, but it’s coming closer to the zero line. This can indicate a decreasing selling volume or momentum. Note that we already saw a bullish engulfing candle last April 9, showing that many buyers already saw new support at the $22-24 range. Lastly, the stock’s RSI is still in the oversold region, which can be a driver of its rebound.

As our trading strategy using a 1:1 Reward-Risk Ratio, take profit at $28.85. Coincidentally, this is almost identical to my P/E TP, giving logic to the recent trading behavior. Meanwhile, stop loss at $18.09.

Conclusion

Live Oak Bancshares, Inc. is a good addition to your portfolio as it still has room for expansion. Its strategic portfolio diversification and focus on commercial banking help it sustain its growth. In addition, it is still cheap with decent returns. Technicals are still weak, but a rebound must be considered. I am giving this stock a buy rating.
